temperature_stages.symptoms: Onset of chills and shivering (rigors); bone-rattling chills lasting 15–60 min as hypothalamus raises thermostat. Mild headache, fatigue. [CDC Malaria Clinical Features; Medmastery Malaria Guide]
Fever rising as body reaches hypothalamic set-point. Chills resolve; headache, myalgia (muscle ache), arthralgia, weakness, nausea and vomiting emerge. [CDC DPDx Malaria; CDC Yellow Book 2026]

Sustained high fever (hot stage); intense headache, flushing, tachycardia, general malaise. Lasts 6–10 hours. Spleen begins to enlarge. [Medmastery Malaria Clinical Guide; CDC Clinical Features of Malaria]

Fever spikes; severe anaemia, jaundice (haemolysis of RBCs), hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, hypoglycaemia. Risk of acute kidney injury. [CDC Malaria; WHO Guidelines for Malaria; CDC Yellow Book 2026]

Cerebral malaria (P. falciparum): seizures, impaired consciousness, coma. Acute respiratory distress, multi-organ failure. Medical emergency — rapid deterioration and death possible. [CDC Clinical Features; WHO Guidelines for Malaria]

Abrupt fever onset after 5–7 day incubation. Facial flushing, erythema, generalized body pain, retro-orbital pain, headache, myalgia, arthralgia, nausea. [PAHO/WHO Dengue; CDC Yellow Book Dengue 2025]

High fever 40–41°C with rash (macular/maculopapular). Minor haemorrhagic signs: petechiae, ecchymosis, epistaxis, bleeding gums. Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia begin. [WHO 2009 Dengue Classification; CDC Yellow Book 2025]

Fever drops to ~38°C — WARNING: highest risk phase. Plasma leakage, shock risk. Warning signs: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hepatomegaly, rising HCT + falling platelets. [CDC Yellow Book 2025; PMC8205005]

Fever resolves; reabsorption of leaked fluids. Risk of fluid overload and bradycardia. Rash may recur (islands of white in sea of red). Appetite returns gradually. [CDC Yellow Book Dengue 2025; WHO Dengue Guidelines 2009]

Biphasic saddleback fever recurs in severe dengue. Severe plasma leakage, dengue shock syndrome, severe haemorrhage (haematemesis, melena), organ impairment (liver, CNS, heart, kidneys). [Medscape Dengue; CDC Yellow Book 2025]

Abrupt fever onset 4–8 days post-bite. Severe arthralgia in small joints (hands, wrists, feet, ankles), headache, myalgia, conjunctivitis. Fever typically >39°C at onset. [WHO CHIKV Fact Sheet 2025; CDC Chikungunya 2024; Wikipedia citing WHO/CDC]

Peak fever; excruciating polyarthritis and polyarthralgia. Maculopapular rash appears on trunk and limbs (approx. day 2–5). Lymph node swelling, severe fatigue, possible nausea. [WHO CHIKV 2025; WHO Arboviral Guidelines 2025 (NBK616300)]

Fever subsides but arthralgia persists or worsens in some patients. Joint stiffness, tenosynovitis, bursitis emerge. Fatigue continues. Rash may resolve. [WHO Arboviral Disease Guidelines 2025; CDC Chikungunya clinical overview]

Persistent inflammatory polyarthritis lasting months to years in up to 60% of patients. Destructive arthritis resembling rheumatoid arthritis in severe cases. Functional disability. [WHO CHIKV Fact Sheet 2025; Lancet / peer-reviewed follow-up studies]

Rare atypical presentations: encephalitis, myocarditis, acute hepatitis, severe bullous dermatosis, haemorrhage. Higher risk in neonates, >65 yrs, and those with comorbidities. [WHO Arboviral Guidelines 2025; WHO CHIKV 2015 case definition — severe acute disease]

Low-grade fever beginning 3–14 days post-bite. Mild malaise, headache. Pruritic maculopapular rash begins on face and spreads to trunk and limbs. [CDC Zika clinical guidance; WHO Arboviral Guidelines 2025]

Non-purulent conjunctivitis (bilateral red eyes), arthralgia in small joints (hands, feet), myalgia, rash peaks. Majority of cases self-resolve within 2–7 days. [WHO Zika Fact Sheet; CDC comparative analysis medrxiv 2025; WHO Arboviral Guidelines 2025]
Fever resolves within 1 week. Rash fades. Mild fatigue and joint discomfort may persist briefly. Most adults recover fully without intervention. [CDC Zika; WHO Zika Fact Sheet 2025]

In pregnant women: even mild or asymptomatic Zika can cause fetal microcephaly, brain malformations, and congenital Zika syndrome regardless of maternal fever severity. Highest risk in 1st trimester. [WHO; CDC; NEJM peer-reviewed studies]

Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) in rare post-infection cases: ascending muscle weakness, paralysis, areflexia. Onset 2–4 weeks after acute illness. Risk ~1 in 4,000 symptomatic infections. [WHO Zika Fact Sheet; peer-reviewed GBS-Zika studies in The Lancet]

symptoms: Jaundice — yellowing of skin and sclera

temperature_stages.symptoms: Sudden onset: fever 39–40°C, chills, headache, dizziness, myalgia, lumbosacral pain. Faget sign: relative bradycardia despite high fever (pulse-temperature dissociation). Facial flushing, conjunctival injection. [MSD Manual; CDC Yellow Fever Clinical Features 2024; NCBI StatPearls NBK470425]

Fever drops suddenly after 3 days — apparent recovery lasting hours to a day. Virus cleared by immune response. ~85% of patients recover permanently at this stage. [CDC Yellow Fever HCP 2024; MSD Manual; Medscape Yellow Fever]

Fever recurs in ~15% of cases. Jaundice, dark urine (bilirubinuria), epigastric pain, profuse vomiting. Hepatorenal syndrome, albuminuria. Transaminases markedly elevated. [CDC Yellow Fever Clinical Features 2024; NCBI NBK470425; MSD Manual]

Haematemesis (black vomit), melena, petechiae, mucosal haemorrhage, epistaxis, oozing from gingiva. DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation). Acute kidney failure, oliguria. [CDC Yellow Fever 2024; Medscape; MSD Manual]

Cardiovascular instability, acute hepatic failure, renal failure, haemorrhagic shock, delirium, seizures, coma. Case fatality 20–60% in severe disease. ICU mandatory. [WHO Yellow Fever Fact Sheet 2025; NCBI NBK470425; MSD Manual]

Approximately 80% of WNV-infected persons have no symptoms after 2–14 day incubation. Subclinical infection; seroconversion occurs silently. [CDC West Nile Virus; Wikipedia citing CDC 2017/2019; PMC3855145]

Fever, headache, body aches, fatigue, nausea, vomiting. Maculopapular rash on trunk in some patients. Swollen lymph nodes. Self-limiting; resolves within days to weeks. [CDC West Nile; Wikipedia citing CDC; PMC3855145]
Prolonged fever, marked fatigue and weakness, significant myalgia. Post-infectious fatigue syndrome possible. Elevated inflammatory markers. [CDC West Nile clinical; PMC3855145 peer review]

West Nile encephalitis: altered consciousness, disorientation, tremors, seizures. West Nile meningitis: neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski signs. Acute flaccid paralysis (polio-like). Risk highest in elderly and immunocompromised. [CDC; PMC3855145; WHO WNV Fact Sheet]

Coma, respiratory failure requiring ventilation. Long-term neurological sequelae in survivors. Mortality ~10% among neuroinvasive cases. [Wikipedia citing CDC; PMC3855145; CDC West Nile neuroinvasive surveillance]

Most infected persons remain permanently asymptomatic. Microfilariae circulate in blood nocturnally. Subclinical lymphatic dilation and dysfunction occur silently. Worm burden accumulates over repeated bites. [CDC Lymphatic Filariasis Clinical Overview; NCBI StatPearls NBK556012]

Episodic fever (38–39°C), chills, headache, myalgia during acute flares triggered by adult worm death. Retrograde lymphangitis (inflamed lymph vessel from node outward toward skin), painful lymphadenopathy. [CDC Lymphatic Filariasis Clinical Overview 2026; NCBI NBK556012]

Secondary bacterial skin infection causing oedematous inflammatory plaques, fever, chills, myalgia. Recurrent ADLA episodes accelerate progression to chronic lymphoedema. [NCBI StatPearls NBK556012; CDC Filarial Worms HCP 2026]

Afebrile between attacks. Progressive, irreversible swelling of limbs, breasts, genitalia (hydrocele). Skin thickening, fibrosis, warty surface changes. Social stigma and functional disability. [CDC Lymphatic Filariasis About Page; WHO NTD Fact Sheet]

Massive non-pitting oedema (elephantiasis) of one or both legs, arms or genitalia. Hardened, lichenified skin. Recurrent bacterial superinfections. WHO-designated leading cause of disability worldwide. Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE) in rare cases. [CDC; WHO; NCBI NBK556012]
